records.culinary_uses: Edible by many field guides when correctly identified and cooked, but slimy and often modest quality. Many people peel the cap skin and discard old or buggy specimens.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.notes: In this guide, Slippery Jack means Suillus luteus: the slimy brown-capped, ringed Suillus usually found with pines. Some foragers use “slippery jack” loosely for other slimy-capped Suillus species, so compare the related Suillus entries before assuming a sticky Suillus is this species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Ringed slippery Suillus with a glutinous brown cap, yellow pores, and pine association. Treat the common name broadly only with caution: this page is for Suillus luteus.
mushroom_profile.ring: Ring present; whitish when young, often purple-brown beneath with age
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer into fall; often with pine.
research_notes: Canonical Slippery Jack record; merged former suillus-luteus duplicate in v4.2.70.

records.edibility_detail: Leccinum species are host-specific and can be difficult to pin down in North America. Record host tree, staining changes over 30 minutes, pore color, and scaber color before using.
records.field_identification: Leccinum-type boletes have pore surfaces and a central stem covered with scabers rather than glandular dots or netting. Host tree is a key identification clue; many are birch, aspen, oak, or other specific-tree associates.
